title: L05 · Present Tense — Regular Verbs
objective: Conjugate any regular Spanish verb across the six personal forms. Master the three families (-ar, -er, -ir) and the personal pronouns. Spanish drops pronouns most of the time — learn when to use them.
strategy: Three steps: (1) memorise the pronouns. (2) drill the three endings tables — they're highly predictable. (3) section 4 teaches when to DROP the pronoun (almost always) and when to keep it (for contrast/emphasis).

sections.title: The six personal pronouns
sections.note: Spanish has more 'you' distinctions than English/French. Plus the regional split: 'vosotros' is used only in Spain, 'ustedes' covers everyone in Latin America.

blocks.translation: you (singular informal) / tu — note the accent (vs 'tu' = your)
he / she / you (formal) / il, elle, vous (formel)
blocks.hint: 'usted' is the formal singular 'you' — for strangers, customers, elders. Conjugates like él/ella (3rd person).
Use 'nosotras' if the group is ALL female. Mixed group = nosotros (masculine wins, sorry).

blocks.translation: you all (informal, Spain only) / vous (informel pluriel)
blocks.hint: Used in Spain for plural informal 'you'. Latin America uses 'ustedes' for ALL plural 'you' (informal + formal).

blocks.translation: they (masc / fem) / you all (formal) / ils, elles, vous (pluriel)
blocks.hint: 'ustedes' is plural formal in Spain, plural ANY (formal + informal) in Latin America. Same conjugation as ellos/ellas.

sections.title: -AR verbs: the biggest family
sections.note: Drop -AR, add: -o, -as, -a, -amos, -áis, -an. About 90% of Spanish verbs end in -ar, including most new/invented ones.

blocks.audioText: hablo, hablas, habla, hablamos, habláis, hablan
blocks.rule: Note the written accent on the vosotros form (-áis). Watch the pattern: yo always -o, tú always -s ending.

examples.translation: I speak Spanish and French. / Je parle espagnol et français.
Do you speak English? / Parles-tu anglais?
They speak very fast. / Ils parlent très vite.
I work in a hospital. / Je travaille dans un hôpital.
We work together. / Nous travaillons ensemble.
I study medicine. / J'étudie la médecine.
What do you study? / Qu'est-ce que tu étudies?

sections.title: -ER verbs
sections.note: Drop -ER, add: -o, -es, -e, -emos, -éis, -en. Pattern is mostly e-flavored versions of -ar endings.

blocks.audioText: como, comes, come, comemos, coméis, comen
blocks.rule: Yo form is always -o (same as -ar). Other endings replace -a with -e.

examples.translation: I eat bread with cheese. / Je mange du pain avec du fromage.
What are you eating today? / Qu'est-ce que tu manges aujourd'hui?
I'm learning Spanish. / J'apprends l'espagnol.
We learn together. / Nous apprenons ensemble.
I read the newspaper every day. / Je lis le journal chaque jour.

sections.title: -IR verbs
sections.note: Drop -IR, add: -o, -es, -e, -imos, -ís, -en. Almost identical to -er, except the nosotros (-imos) and vosotros (-ís) forms.

blocks.audioText: vivo, vives, vive, vivimos, vivís, viven
blocks.rule: Yo, tú, él, ellos forms are identical to -er. Only nosotros/vosotros differ.

examples.translation: I live in Madrid. / Je vis à Madrid.
Where do you live? / Où vis-tu?

examples.text: Vivimos en España desde hace 5 años.
examples.audioText: Vivimos en España desde hace 5 años.
examples.translation: We've been living in Spain for 5 years. / Nous vivons en Espagne depuis 5 ans.
I'm writing a book. / J'écris un livre.
We write to our grandmother. / Nous écrivons à notre grand-mère.

sections.title: Drop the pronoun (most of the time)
sections.note: This is what makes Spanish sound natural. Because each verb form is UNIQUE (yo hablo, tú hablas, etc.), Spanish doesn't need to say 'yo' or 'tú' — the verb ending already tells you who.

blocks.text: Default: drop the subject pronoun unless you need it for emphasis or contrast
blocks.audioText: Hablo español. Comemos a las ocho. Vive en Madrid.
blocks.rule: Spanish is a 'pro-drop' language — the verb ending tells you the subject, so the pronoun is redundant. Always-drop is more natural than always-keep. Italian and Portuguese work the same way; French does NOT (you must say 'je parle', not just 'parle').

examples.translation: I speak Spanish. / Je parle espagnol. — no 'yo' needed
We eat at 8. / Nous mangeons à 8h.
He/she lives in Madrid. / Il/elle vit à Madrid. — context tells you who 'vive' refers to

blocks.text: KEEP the pronoun for: contrast, emphasis, ambiguity
blocks.audioText: Yo hablo español, pero ella habla francés. Él es médico.
blocks.rule: (1) Contrast between two subjects: 'Yo hablo X, ella habla Y'. (2) Emphasis: 'TÚ eres mi mejor amigo'. (3) When the verb form alone is ambiguous (e.g. él/ella/usted all share one ending) and context doesn't make it clear.

examples.text: Yo hablo español, pero ella habla francés.
examples.audioText: Yo hablo español, pero ella habla francés.
examples.translation: I speak Spanish, but she speaks French. / Je parle espagnol, mais elle parle français.

examples.text: Él es médico, ella es profesora.
examples.audioText: Él es médico, ella es profesora.
examples.translation: He's a doctor, she's a teacher. / Il est médecin, elle est professeur.
YOU decide. (emphasis) / C'est toi qui décides.
